moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Trompowsky Attack, an aggressive system where White develops the bishop to g5 on move 2, avoiding the main lines of the Queen's Gambit. White plays 1.d4, claiming the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: A single central pawn on d4 controls e5 and c5. White keeps the c-pawn and e-pawn flexible for now.
plans: Follow up with Bg5 to enter the Trompowsky Attack
Control the dark squares in the center
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4, then 2.Bg5 — the Trompowsky, avoiding Queen's Gambit lines.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops Nf6, the most common response to 1.d4. The Nf6 controls e4 and d5 — but in the Trompowsky, this knight becomes an immediate target.
plans: Control e4 and prepare ...d5 or ...g6
Develop naturally toward kingside castling
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Nf6 attacks e4 and d5, but in the Trompowsky it becomes an immediate target.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays Bg5, the Trompowsky Attack. The bishop pins the Nf6 to the queen, creating immediate tactical pressure. This is the defining move — White avoids 2.c4 (Queen's Gambit) and creates an early imbalance.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The pawn structure is still fluid. White's plan with Bg5 is to provoke structural weaknesses in Bl.
plans: Pressure the f6 knight — trade it to double Black's pawns, or force it to move
Avoid main-line theory and steer the game into less familiar territory
alternatives: Nf3 — the classical approach, entering the main lines of d4 theory
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bg5 pins the knight to the queen, creating immediate tactical pressure and an early imbalance.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The 2...d5 Bxf6 Line is an important variation of the Trompowsky Attack. Taking on f6 gives Black doubled pawns but the bishop pair. White plays a solid setup with e3, Bd3, and short castle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the Trompowsky, 2...d5 Bxf6 gives Black doubled pawns but the bishop pair; White castles short with e3 and Bd3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the 2...d5 Bxf6 Line — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Nf6 forces the 2...d5 Bxf6 line, shaping the pawn structure and plans.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black challenges your central d4 pawn immediately, forcing you to decide whether to capture on f6 or maintain tension. This move tests whether the Trompowsky's pin justifies White's unusual setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d5 stakes a central claim, controlling space and restricting White's piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Raptor Variation (2...c5) is an important variation of the Trompowsky Attack. The Raptor Variation arises after 2...c5 3.d5 Qb6. White gambits the b2 pawn for rapid development and a huge center.
